title: Chapter 31: The Body Remembers
content: Day twenty.

I haven't slept in three. The hum stopped being a vibration sometime around hour sixty — it's a drone now, constant and low, tinnitus wrapped around the base of my skull like wire. Like a generator running somewhere behind my thoughts, consuming fuel I didn't know I had left. I can hear it when I breathe. I can hear it when I don't.

I'm pinning the sleeve of Look Eleven — the deconstructed bomber, the one with the raw selvedge edges and the hand-stitched facing that took me nineteen hours. The silk-cotton blend slides between my fingers and I reach for a pin and my hand blurs. Not from exhaustion. Not from the tremor that's been living in my wrists since day fourteen.
My skin shimmers.

It's quick — less than a second — a rippling transparency that starts at my knuckles and washes up toward my wrist like heat off asphalt. I see the faint architecture of bone beneath, the shadow of metacarpals, and then it's gone. My hand is solid again. My hand is a hand again.
I drop the pin. It tinks across the studio floor and disappears into the scatter of fabric scraps and thread tails and chalk dust that has become my world.
I pick up another pin. I pin the sleeve. My fingers cooperate, but they don't feel like mine. They feel borrowed. They feel like gloves I'm wearing someone else's hands inside of.

The studio air is thick — chalk and sweat and the chemical bite of fabric sizing. I've been breathing it so long I don't taste anything else. My tongue is cotton. My throat is sandpaper. I should drink water. I should eat. I should sleep.
I pick up another pin.

The drone hums. The cells beneath my skin hum with it, restless, shifting in their seats like an audience waiting for the curtain. They know something I've been refusing to name. They've been waiting longer than I have.
The door opens.

I don't look up. I hear the rustle of a paper bag, the squeak of sneakers on concrete, and then the sharp intake of breath that tells me Suki has seen me. Actually seen me. Not the version I've been projecting through texts and door cracks and the lie of "I'm fine, I'm almost done, I just need to finish this one piece."
"Orlando."
Her voice is careful. The way you'd speak to something wounded. The way you'd approach something that might bolt.
I look up. The studio spins — the industrial lights overhead smearing into streaks, the bolts of fabric on their shelves tilting like ships. I blink hard and the world reassembles itself, barely.

Suki is standing in the doorway with a takeout bag in one hand and a paper drink carrier in the other. Her dark hair is pulled back in a loose knot and she's wearing that oversized NYU sweatshirt she refuses to retire and her expression is doing something complicated — concern and anger and something underneath both that I don't have the bandwidth to decode.
"You look like shit," she says.
"I'm fine."
"You haven't answered your phone in two days."
"I was working."
"You look like shit, Orlando."

I open my mouth to argue and the hum shifts pitch — drops, actually, like a engine winding down, and my hands do the shimmer thing again, both of them this time, the transparency rippling from fingertips to wrists and back in a wave. I watch it happen. I watch my own hands become temporarily, terrifyingly, see-through.

I'm on the floor. I don't remember sitting down. I think my knees just stopped believing in me. I'm surrounded by fabric and pins and the scattered evidence of twenty days of creative fever — thread snips and chalk shards and crumpled pattern paper and empty coffee cups that have become small monuments to my inability to stop.
I'm staring at my hands.

Not resting. Staring. Because they look wrong. The knuckles are wider than they were last week — I can see it, I can feel it when I flex my fingers, the joints sitting thicker, heavier, like the bones are expanding from the inside. My finger joints have changed too. The proportions are off. The architecture of my hands — the hands I've used for four centuries to cut and drape and pin and stitch — is shifting.
"It's starting," I say.
Suki drops the takeout bag.

It hits the concrete with a muffled thump, the containers inside shifting, and she doesn't even look at it. Her eyes are fixed on my hands. On the way my fingers are still trembling, still shimmering faintly at the edges, like there's electricity trapped just below the skin.
"What's starting?" Her voice is controlled. Too controlled. The kind of controlled that means she's already guessed the answer.
"The shift." I hear myself say it and the word makes it real — makes it a thing that's happening instead of a thing I've been running from. "It's coming early. I can feel it."

Suki is quiet for a long moment. Then she crosses the studio, steps over a drift of muslin, and sits down beside me on the floor. She doesn't touch me — she's learned that by now, learned that unexpected contact sends the hum into overdrive, makes my cells accelerate toward the shift like a car skidding on ice. She sits close enough that I can smell her — cedar and something floral, her shampoo, the cold outside air still clinging to her clothes — and she pulls her knees up and she looks at me.
"Okay," she says. "What do you need?"

The question is so simple it hurts. What do I need. What do I need. I need to not be changing. I need my hands to stay my hands. I need the hum to stop. I need four hundred years of memory to not feel like it's living in a body that's about to evict me.
"I need to finish the collection."
"Orlando."

"I'm serious." I flex my fingers — the new, thicker joints, the wider knuckles — and feel the wrongness of it like a note sung off-key. "If the shift happens before the showcase, I won't be able to present. This body made those garments. These hands cut that fabric. These fingers shaped every seam and every dart and every rolled hem. A different body won't have the same hands."

I hold them up — my hands, my changing hands — and the studio light catches them and for a moment I can see through myself again, the shimmer translucent and strange, and I pull them back against my chest like I can hide them.

"A different body won't know the collection like I do," I say, quieter. "The muscle memory won't translate. The instincts won't carry over. I'll be someone else wearing borrowed clothes and pretending I know what they mean."

Suki is watching me with those dark, steady eyes. The overhead light casts her face half in shadow and I can see the muscle in her jaw working, the way she's choosing her words like she's picking up something fragile.
"So you're going to work yourself into a shift," she says. It's not a question.
"I'm going to work myself into a showcase." I meet her eyes. "The shift is going to happen either way."

The drone wraps around the words, thickens them, makes them feel like they're coming from somewhere deeper than my throat. Somewhere cellular. Somewhere the hum is already composing the next movement.
"Either way," I repeat, because I need to hear it twice. Because I need it to be true. "The only variable is whether I finish first."
"The only variable," Suki says, and now her voice has an edge on it, a blade sliding out from beneath the softness, "is whether you survive to present at all."

She shifts on the concrete, turning to face me fully. Her eyes are fierce — not the concern from a moment ago, not the careful tenderness. This is something hotter. This is the Suki who once told me she doesn't do halfway, who planted herself in my life like a flag and refused to leave, who looked at four centuries of loneliness and said "not anymore."

"Listen to me," she says. "You told me you've been alone for four hundred years. You told me that like it was just a fact, like it was the weather. Like isolation was something that happened to you instead of something you chose over and over because leaving was easier than staying."
My chest tightens. The hum pulses in response. She keeps going.

"But you're not alone now." She jabs a finger at the floor between us. "I'm right here. I brought you noodles. I'm sitting on your disgusting studio floor at — what time is it — eleven at night because you stopped answering your phone. And I am not going to watch you destroy yourself for a fashion show."

"It's not a fashion show." The words come out harder than I intend, sharper, and the hum pitches up and my skin flickers again — my forearms this time, the transparency rippling up past my elbows before it settles. I don't look at it. I look at Suki. "It's my life. Everything I've ever been — every body, every face, every name — all of it leads to that runway. If I can't show the world what I am through my work, if I can't stand there and let the garments speak for what four hundred years of fluidity looks like, then Maren wins by default."
Suki doesn't flinch at the name. She just watches me, and the silence stretches between us like fabric on a bolt, like something waiting to be cut.
"And if you shift before the showcase and can't present at all?" she asks. Quiet. Reasonable. Devastating. "Then Maren wins anyway."

The words land somewhere below my ribs and stay there, heavy and hot. Because she's right. Because the logic I've been using to justify this — the idea that working harder, pushing further, burning through the last reserves of this body will somehow result in victory — is a logic built on sand. The showcase is the battlefield. But if I shift before I reach it, I won't be a soldier. I'll be a stranger in unfamiliar armor, fumbling with weapons I've never held.
"So eat the damn noodles," Suki says, "and go to sleep."

She reaches over — slowly, telegraphing the movement — and picks up the takeout bag from the floor. She pulls out a container, pops the lid. The smell hits me: thai basil and garlic and the salty-sweet warmth of pad kee mao, and my stomach cramps so hard I almost double over. I haven't eaten since — I don't remember. Yesterday? The day before?
She holds the container out to me.
I take it.

The noodles are cold — they've been sitting in the bag since she arrived, and the journey across the studio floor didn't help — but they taste like the first thing I've eaten in a year. The basil is sharp on my tongue, the chili oil bright and stinging, the rice noodles soft and yielding. I eat standing still, I mean sitting still, I mean I eat without moving anything except my jaw and my hand, and the taste grounds me in a way that nothing else has in days. A flavor. A specific, human, ordinary flavor. Not the metallic hum or the chalk-dust air or the chemical bite of fabric sizing — food. Someone brought me food.

I eat the whole container. Suki watches without comment. When I'm done, she hands me a drink — iced tea, too sweet, orange — and I drink that too, the sugar blooming through my bloodstream like a small, temporary sunrise.
Then I lie down.

I don't mean to. It just happens — my body deciding for me, my spine meeting the studio floor, my cheek pressing against a remnant of ivory silk that's been waiting there like it planned this. The fabric is cool and smooth against my skin. It smells like the studio — chalk and cotton and the mineral ghost of steam from the pressing station — and underneath all of that, it smells like me. Like the hours I've spent here. Like the body that's been dissolving slowly into its next shape.

I close my eyes. The hum doesn't stop — it never stops — but it settles, recedes to the background like music in another room. I can feel the silk under my cheek. I can hear Suki moving quietly, gathering the empty containers, the scattered pins, the worst of the debris. I can smell the faint lingering ghost of thai basil.
"Hey," I murmur, my voice already drifting, already halfway under. "Thank you."
"Eat your noodles next time I call," she says. "And I won't have to thank me."

I hear the soft sound of her sitting down again — close, watchful, a presence at the edge of my unraveling. I hear the quiet pop of her chewing gum, the faint click of her phone screen. I hear — or maybe I imagine — the small, sharp exhale she makes when she's worried and trying not to show it.
The studio is dim now. She must have turned off the overheads. The only light is the emergency exit sign, red and steady, and the glow of her phone screen washing her face in blue.
I sleep.

It's not good sleep. It's not rest. It's a thin, restless thing, a skim across the surface of unconsciousness, and the hum dreams for me. The hum has been dreaming for days now — filling the spaces between waking with images I didn't choose, narratives I didn't author. The drone becomes a runway, the runway becomes a body, the body becomes—

I'm standing on the catwalk. The lights are hot and white and they press against my skin like a hand. I'm wearing something — I don't know what — and my feet are in shoes I've never seen, platforms that lift me higher than I should be, and I look down at my hands and they're not my hands. The knuckles are wider. The joints are thicker. The skin shimmers and shifts and I watch my own fingers become someone else's, watch the transparency ripple up past my wrists, past my elbows, and I lift my head and the audience is—
Screaming.

Not applause. Not admiration. Screaming. The way you scream at something you don't recognize, something that shouldn't exist, something that's becoming in front of you in real time. Their mouths are open and their eyes are wide and the sound is enormous, a wall of terror and fascination, and I open my mouth to say "it's still me" but the voice that comes out isn't mine either.
I wake up with my hands clenched and the hum roaring at full pitch, my skin flickering from throat to temple, and for three full seconds I don't know which body I'm in.

Then I feel the silk under my cheek. I hear Suki's gum-popping and her quiet, steady breathing. I smell thai basil and chalk dust. And I know — I know with the desperate certainty of someone who can feel the clock in her own cells — that the shift is coming early.
My hands are already changing.

The showcase — the battlefield I chose, the stage where four hundred years of becoming was supposed to crystallize into something visible, something undeniable, something that would make Maren's erasure impossible — is two weeks away.
Two weeks.

The question isn't whether I'll shift. That drum is already beating, the cells already counting down, the hum already writing the orchestration of my next becoming. The question is whether I'll still be myself when the lights go up. Whether the hands that made the collection will be the hands that present it. Whether Orlando — this Orlando, the one who fought and built and bled for every garment in this room — will still exist behind whatever face the shift gives me next.
The hum drones on. It already knows the answer. It's just waiting for me to hear it.
